renaissance.name: Renaissance
renaissance.description: A period of 'rebirth' in arts and culture. Music became increasingly polyphonic, with growing importance of secular forms alongside sacred music. The printing press enabled wider distribution of musical scores.

baroque.name: Baroque
baroque.description: An era of grandeur and ornamentation. The birth of opera, the development of tonality, the invention of the concerto and sonata. Composers began writing more idiomatic instrumental music, and the basso continuo became the foundation of ensemble playing.

classical.name: Classical
classical.description: Clarity, balance, and structural elegance define this era. The symphony, string quartet, and piano sonata became the dominant forms. Music moved from aristocratic courts to public concert halls.

early-romantic.name: Early Romantic
early-romantic.description: Emotion, individualism, and poetic expression take center stage. Composers pushed harmonic boundaries and explored programmatic music — telling stories through sound. The piano became the dominant solo instrument.

late-romantic.name: Late Romantic
late-romantic.description: Massive orchestral forces, extreme chromaticism, and nationalist movements characterize the Late Romantic. Composers built vast symphonic structures and dramatic operas. Musical nationalism brought folk traditions into the concert hall.

modern.name: Modern
modern.description: A fracturing of tradition into multiple movements: Impressionism, Expressionism, Neoclassicism, Serialism, and beyond. Composers rejected or radically extended Romantic conventions, experimenting with new scales, rhythms, timbres, and forms.
